peer_exemplars.reasoning: High openness to experience and a preference for participatory democracy (GAL-TAN participatory democracy at 39.24) combine with a strong system-justification orientation (92.22) to produce moderate support for citizen assemblies as a deliberative complement to existing institutions.

High openness and very high conscientiousness, combined with strong populist people-centrism and participatory democracy preferences, drive moderate support for citizen assemblies as a deliberative tool, though low trust in institutions tempers enthusiasm.
High openness and extraversion support participatory democracy, but low agreeableness and high anti-elitism temper enthusiasm for deliberative bodies.

Very low openness to experience and high authoritarianism make participatory democracy feel inefficient and destabilizing, but high agreeableness and populist anti-elitism yield moderate rather than maximal opposition.

Moderate openness to experience and a preference for participatory democracy (GAL-TAN participatory democracy 53.91) provide mild support, but low agreeableness and high social dominance orientation (88.54) temper enthusiasm for collective decision-making.

High openness and libertarian civil liberties support favor participatory democracy, but low GAL-TAN green-alternative-libertarian scores and high system justification temper enthusiasm for replacing representative structures.

Very low openness and low GAL-TAN green-libertarian scores (3.84%) indicate deep skepticism toward participatory democratic innovations, while high authoritarian submission (71.55%) and conventionalism (84.84%) favor established hierarchical decision-making over citizen-led assemblies.
High openness and participatory democracy preference (60.78) drive moderate support for citizen assemblies, but low trust in institutions tempers enthusiasm.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Extremely high political trust (97.99), very high system justification (92.22), and strong conventionalism (99.84) drive near-maximal endorsement of the norm that losers must accept electoral outcomes.

Very high system justification and political system justification, alongside strong civic nationalism and high dutifulness, produce clear support for the norm of loser consent, but low external political efficacy and low trust in government slightly reduce the score from maximal.
Strong support for democratic norms due to high trust in institutions and low collective narcissism, but moderate system justification tempers extreme endorsement.
High agreeableness and strong preference for social harmony are offset by very low political trust and a fearful-avoidant attachment style that suspects the system will not reciprocate fairness.
High system justification (81.78) and political trust (41.34) support procedural legitimacy, yet low authoritarian submission (3.08) and a history of justice system contact reduce full endorsement.
High agreeableness, dutifulness, and system justification support accepting electoral outcomes, though a history of legal system contact and moderate external efficacy introduce slight skepticism.

High authoritarian submission (71.55%) and conventionalism (84.84%) support orderly acceptance of electoral outcomes, but low trust in government (8.95%) and low external political efficacy (60.62%) temper this into moderate rather than strong endorsement.
Strong fairness/equity values (0.75) and high internal political efficacy (89.34) support loser consent, though high collective narcissism (89.11) introduces some resistance.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Very high authoritarian aggression (96.91) and conventionalism (99.84) combined with high trust in government (91.7) produce categorical support for peaceful transfers as a sacred procedural norm.

High trust in political system and low acceptance of political violence drive strong support, though high social dominance orientation slightly reduces enthusiasm.

High authoritarian law-and-order orientation and the value of predictability strongly support peaceful transitions, but low system justification and past criminal-justice experience temper full endorsement.
Strong system justification (81.78) and high political efficacy (70.61) align with this norm, though low agreeableness (36.89) and past exposure to violence/harassment introduce slight skepticism.
Very high conscientiousness, high dutifulness, and strong conventionalism from RWA strongly endorse orderly transitions, while low dangerous-worldview reduces fear-driven exceptions.
High conventionalism (84.84%) and respect for tradition/rank (97.32%) strongly favor procedural continuity, though low trust in institutions (8.11% mean) prevents maximal support.
High dutifulness (96.25) and moderate trust in government (67.99) support peaceful transfers, but a history of domestic violence exposure and criminal record create slight ambivalence.

Very high system justification (96.05) and political system justification (93.16), combined with high conscientiousness and strong rule-of-law orientation, produce clear support for judicial independence, despite low personal trust in the judiciary.
High trust in judiciary and low authority subversion support independence, while high populist anti-elitism adds skepticism of judicial power.
Profound distrust in judiciary makes protecting its independence feel like shielding an untrusted institution from democratic accountability, despite high abstract support for rule of law.
High system justification (81.78) and respect for authority (68.29) support judicial independence, though low trust in judiciary (36.75) and personal justice system contact create some ambivalence.

High respect for authority (96.84) and moderate trust in judiciary (51.79) support judicial independence, while high disruptor tendencies (83.37) create some skepticism.
